docs.title: 1. Core concepts
docs.text: Leafmap is a Python package for interactive mapping and geospatial analysis in Jupyter environments. It supports multiple plotting backends, with ipyleaflet as the default backend in standard notebook workflows and folium in some environments.

docs.title: 1.1 Main ideas
docs.text:
• Map: the main interactive map object.
  • Backends: different rendering engines such as ipyleaflet, folium, plotly, pydeck, kepler.gl, and heremap.
  • Local data support: vector, raster, and tabular geospatial files.
  • Interactive tools: drawing, splitting, querying, and editing data.
  • Export tools: save maps, images, animations, and vector outputs.

docs.title: 1.2 Common imports
docs.text: import leafmap

docs.title: 1.3 Basic map example
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map(center=(40, -100), zoom=4)
m

docs.title: 2. Mapping backends
docs.text: Leafmap supports several backends, and each offers a different balance of interactivity and portability.

docs.title: 2.1 Common backend imports
docs.text:
• import leafmap - Default backend, usually ipyleaflet.
  • import leafmap.leafmap as leafmap - Explicit ipyleaflet backend.
  • import leafmap.foliumap as leafmap - Folium backend.
  • import leafmap.plotlymap as leafmap - Plotly backend.
  • import leafmap.deck as leafmap - Pydeck backend.
  • import leafmap.kepler as leafmap - Kepler.gl backend.
  • import leafmap.heremap as leafmap - Here map backend.

docs.title: 2.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Use ipyleaflet for the richest interactive notebook experience.
  • Use folium for lightweight HTML map sharing.
  • Use plotly for combined chart and map analysis.
  • Use kepler.gl or pydeck for advanced large-data visualization.

docs.title: 2.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap.leafmap as leafmap

m = leafmap.Map(center=(40, -100), zoom=4)
m

docs.title: 3. Map creation
docs.text: Leafmap map objects allow you to create interactive maps, set the view, and control toolbar and widget behavior.

docs.title: 3.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• leafmap.Map() - Create an interactive map.
  • Map.set_center() - Set center and zoom level.
  • Map.center_object() - Center the map on a geometry or dataset.
  • Map.add_basemap() - Add a basemap.
  • Map.add_layer_control() - Add a layer control widget.
  • Map.add_legend() - Add a categorical legend.
  • Map.add_colorbar() - Add a colorbar.
  • Map.add_html() - Add custom HTML content.
  • Map.add_widget() - Add widgets to the map.
  • Map.clear_controls() - Clear controls from the map.

docs.title: 3.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Create a base map for spatial exploration.
  • Set the view to an AOI or study region.
  • Add legends and controls for communication.

docs.title: 3.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map(center=(52.52, 13.405), zoom=10)
m.add_basemap("OpenStreetMap")
m.add_layer_control()
m

docs.title: 4. Basemaps and tiles
docs.text: Leafmap includes many built-in basemaps and supports custom tile services.

docs.title: 4.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• Map.add_basemap() - Add a named basemap.
  • Map.add_xyz_service() - Add XYZ tile service.
  • Map.add_wms_layer() - Add WMS layers.
  • Map.add_cog_layer() - Add a Cloud Optimized GeoTIFF layer.
  • Map.add_raster() - Add raster data.
  • Map.add_tile_layer() - Add custom tile layers.

docs.title: 4.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Add satellite imagery or terrain backgrounds.
  • Overlay external map services.
  • Display remote sensing rasters.

docs.title: 4.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map()
m.add_basemap("Esri.WorldImagery")
m.add_basemap("OpenTopoMap")
m

docs.title: 5. Vector data
docs.text: Leafmap supports reading, displaying, styling, and exporting vector data in many formats.

docs.title: 5.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• Map.add_vector() - Add a vector layer.
  • Map.add_gdf() - Add a GeoDataFrame.
  • Map.add_geojson() - Add GeoJSON.
  • Map.add_shp() - Add shapefile.
  • Map.add_kml() - Add KML.
  • Map.add_csv() - Add CSV data.
  • Map.add_points_from_xy() - Add points from coordinates.
  • Map.add_marker_cluster() - Add clustered markers.
  • Map.add_circle_markers() - Add circle markers.
  • Map.add_labels() - Add labels to vector data.

docs.title: 5.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Display boundaries, survey points, and study sites.
  • Convert tabular coordinate data into a map layer.
  • Cluster dense point datasets for readability.

docs.title: 5.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map()
# m.add_geojson("boundary.geojson", layer_name="Boundary")
m

docs.title: 6. Raster data
docs.text: Leafmap is useful for visualizing raster datasets such as GeoTIFF, COG, and multiband imagery.

docs.title: 6.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• Map.add_raster() - Add raster layers.
  • Map.add_cog_layer() - Add Cloud Optimized GeoTIFF layers.
  • Map.add_stac_layer() - Add STAC items.
  • Map.add_tile_layer() - Add raster-like tiles.
  • Map.add_image() - Add image overlays.
  • Map.add_video() - Add video overlays.

docs.title: 6.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Display elevation, land cover, or remote sensing products.
  • Compare rasters visually.
  • Share web maps with raster overlays.

docs.title: 6.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map()
# m.add_raster("dem.tif", layer_name="DEM")
m

docs.title: 7. Styling layers
docs.text: Leafmap provides styling tools for vector and raster visualization.

docs.title: 7.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• Map.add_style() - Apply style settings to layers.
  • Map.add_vector() - Add styled vectors.
  • Map.add_legend() - Add legend for classes.
  • Map.add_colorbar() - Add continuous colorbar.
  • Map.set_plot_options() - Adjust plotting behavior in some workflows.
  • Map.add_outline() - Add outlines to geometries.

docs.title: 7.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Apply symbology for thematic maps.
  • Highlight polygons and labels.
  • Create publication-ready visual design.

docs.title: 7.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map()
m.add_legend(title="Land Cover", labels=["Forest", "Water"], colors=["green", "blue"])
m

docs.title: 8. Interactive tools
docs.text: Leafmap includes interactive tools for drawing, measuring, splitting, and inspecting maps.

docs.title: 8.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• Map.add_draw_control() - Add drawing tools.
  • Map.add_measure_control() - Add measurement tools.
  • Map.add_toolbar() - Add a toolbar.
  • Map.split_map() - Compare two basemaps or layers side by side.
  • Map.add_search_control() - Add search functionality.
  • Map.add_inspector() - Inspect map values or layers.
  • Map.get_drawn_features() - Get drawn geometries.
  • Map.clear_draw_control() - Clear drawings.

docs.title: 8.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Digitize polygons interactively.
  • Measure distances and areas.
  • Compare before/after imagery with split maps.

docs.title: 8.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map()
m.add_draw_control()
m.add_measure_control()
m

docs.title: 9. Earth Engine support
docs.text: Leafmap can also connect to Google Earth Engine for cloud-based geospatial data visualization and analysis.

docs.title: 9.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• Map.add_ee_layer() - Add Earth Engine layer.
  • Map.center_object() - Center on an Earth Engine object.
  • Map.ee_search() - Search EE assets or datasets.
  • Map.add_time_slider() - Add time slider for imagery.
  • Map.ts_inspector() - Time series inspection.
  • Map.extract_values_to_points() - Sample values at points.
  • Map.zonal_statistics() - Compute zonal statistics.

docs.title: 9.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Display Earth Engine imagery.
  • Run time-based visual analysis.
  • Summarize imagery by polygon zones.

docs.title: 9.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap
import ee

ee.Initialize()

m = leafmap.Map(center=(40, -100), zoom=4)
image = ee.Image("USGS/SRTMGL1_003")
m.add_ee_layer(image, {"min": 0, "max": 4000}, "DEM")
m

docs.title: 10. Data conversion utilities
docs.text: Leafmap includes helper functions for converting between local geospatial formats and Earth Engine-compatible objects.

docs.title: 10.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• geojson_to_ee() - Convert GeoJSON to Earth Engine.
  • shp_to_ee() - Convert shapefile to Earth Engine.
  • kml_to_ee() - Convert KML to Earth Engine.
  • csv_to_ee() - Convert CSV to Earth Engine.
  • gdf_to_ee() - Convert GeoDataFrame to Earth Engine.
  • vector_to_geojson() - Convert vector to GeoJSON.
  • points_from_xy() - Create points from x/y coordinates.

docs.title: 10.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Move local data into cloud workflows.
  • Prepare survey data for mapping.
  • Convert tabular spatial data to features.

docs.title: 10.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap

# ee_object = leafmap.gdf_to_ee(gdf)

docs.title: 11. Export tools
docs.text: Leafmap supports saving maps and exporting spatial outputs for sharing and reporting.

docs.title: 11.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• Map.to_html() - Export map to HTML.
  • Map.save() - Save map output.
  • Map.screenshot() - Capture a screenshot.
  • Map.to_image() - Export a static image when supported.
  • Map.export_vector() - Export vector data.
  • Map.export_image() - Export raster or EE image outputs.
  • Map.export_video() - Export animations.
  • Map.add_gif() - Add GIF animations.

docs.title: 11.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Publish an interactive web map.
  • Save a report figure.
  • Export animations for presentations.

docs.title: 11.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map()
m.to_html("leafmap.html")

docs.title: 12. Charts and plots
docs.text: Leafmap can integrate plotting workflows with interactive spatial analysis.

docs.title: 12.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• Map.plot() - Plot data on the map or linked visualization.
  • Map.chart() - Create charts in supported workflows.
  • Map.histogram() - Show histograms.
  • Map.add_plot() - Add a plot widget.
  • Map.add_colorbar() - Add continuous scale.

docs.title: 12.2 Use case
docs.text:
• Explore data distributions.
  • Build map-linked charts.
  • Visualize attribute changes across regions.

docs.title: 12.3 Example
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map()
# m.histogram(data)
m

docs.title: 13.1 Create a basic map
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map(center=(40, -100), zoom=4)
m.add_basemap("OpenStreetMap")
m.add_layer_control()
m

docs.title: 13.2 Add vector and raster layers
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map()
# m.add_vector("boundary.geojson")
# m.add_raster("elevation.tif")
m

docs.title: 13.3 Split map comparison
docs.text:
import leafmap

m = leafmap.Map()
m.split_map(left_layer="OpenStreetMap", right_layer="Esri.WorldImagery")
m

docs.title: 13.4 Export map
docs.text: m.to_html("output.html")

docs.title: 14. Quick reference table
docs.text: Area Key methods Purpose Map creation Map(), set_center(), center_object() Create and control maps. Basemaps add_basemap(), add_xyz_service(), add_wms_layer() Add background layers. Vector data add_vector(), add_geojson(), add_gdf() Visualize vector data. Raster data add_raster(), add_cog_layer() Visualize rasters. Interactive tools add_draw_control(), add_measure_control(), split_map() Explore and compare. Earth Engine add_ee_layer(), zonal_statistics() Use EE datasets and analysis. Export to_html(), export_image(), export_vector() Save results. Styling add_legend(), add_colorbar() Improve map readability.

docs.title: 15. Example full workflow
docs.text:
import leafmap
import ee

ee.Initialize()

m = leafmap.Map(center=(40, -100), zoom=4)
image = ee.Image("USGS/SRTMGL1_003")
m.add_ee_layer(image, {"min": 0, "max": 4000, "palette": ["blue", "cyan", "green", "yellow", "red"]}, "Elevation")
m.add_basemap("Esri.WorldImagery")
m.add_legend(title="Elevation", labels=["Low", "High"], colors=["blue", "red"])
m.add_layer_control()
m.to_html("leafmap_dem.html")

docs.title: 1. Core concepts
docs.text: Matplotlib provides both a state-based pyplot interface and an object-oriented interface built around Figure and Axes objects. The object-oriented approach is the recommended pattern when more control and customization are needed.

docs.title: 1.1 Main objects
docs.text:
• Figure: the top-level container for everything drawn in a plot.
  • Axes: the plotting area where most data, labels, scales, and annotations are added.
  • Artist: everything visible on a figure, including lines, text, axes, legends, and patches.
  • Canvas: the rendering target where artists are drawn.

docs.title: 1.2 Common import patterns
docs.text:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib as mpl

docs.title: 1.3 Recommended object-oriented pattern
docs.text:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 200)
y = np.sin(x)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, y)
ax.set_title("Sine wave")
ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("sin(x)")
plt.show()

docs.title: 2. Figure creation
docs.text: Figures can be created directly with plt.figure() or more commonly with plt.subplots(), which returns both the Figure and one or more Axes objects.

docs.title: 2.1 Core figure methods
docs.text:
• plt.figure() - Create a new empty figure.
  • plt.subplots() - Create a figure and a grid of subplots in one call.
  • fig.add_axes() - Add axes at an explicit position in figure coordinates.
  • fig.add_subplot() - Add a subplot by grid position.
  • fig.subplots() - Create subplots from an existing figure.
  • fig.suptitle() - Add a figure-level title.
  • fig.text() - Add text in figure coordinates.
  • fig.legend() - Add a figure-level legend.
  • fig.colorbar() - Add a colorbar linked to a mappable artist.
  • fig.savefig() - Save the figure to a file.
  • fig.set_facecolor() - Set figure background color.
  • plt.close() - Close a figure and release memory.

docs.title: 2.2 Common signatures
docs.text:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5), dpi=100)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(nrows=2, ncols=2, figsize=(10, 6), layout="constrained")
fig.add_axes([0.1, 0.1, 0.8, 0.8])

docs.title: 3. Axes basics
docs.text: The Axes object is the main gateway to plotting methods, axis labels, titles, limits, scales, and styling. Most plotting work happens through methods called on ax.

docs.title: 3.1 Core axes methods
docs.text:
• ax.plot() - Plot lines or markers.
  • ax.scatter() - Plot point markers.
  • ax.bar() - Plot vertical bars.
  • ax.barh() - Plot horizontal bars.
  • ax.hist() - Plot histograms.
  • ax.imshow() - Display image or raster-like arrays.
  • ax.contour() - Draw contour lines.
  • ax.contourf() - Draw filled contours.
  • ax.boxplot() - Draw boxplots.
  • ax.violinplot() - Draw violin plots.
  • ax.errorbar() - Plot data with error bars.
  • ax.fill_between() - Fill area between curves.
  • ax.stem() - Plot stem charts.
  • ax.step() - Plot step lines.
  • ax.stackplot() - Plot stacked area charts.
  • ax.pie() - Plot a pie chart.

docs.title: 3.2 Titles and labels
docs.text:
• ax.set_title() - Set axes title.
  • ax.set_xlabel() - Set x-axis label.
  • ax.set_ylabel() - Set y-axis label.
  • ax.set() - Set multiple properties in one call.

docs.title: 3.3 Limits and scales
docs.text:
• ax.set_xlim() - Set x limits.
  • ax.set_ylim() - Set y limits.
  • ax.set_xscale() - Set x scale, such as linear or log.
  • ax.set_yscale() - Set y scale.
  • ax.invert_xaxis() - Reverse x direction.
  • ax.invert_yaxis() - Reverse y direction.

docs.title: 3.4 Ticks and grids
docs.text:
• ax.set_xticks() - Set x tick positions.
  • ax.set_yticks() - Set y tick positions.
  • ax.set_xticklabels() - Set x tick labels.
  • ax.set_yticklabels() - Set y tick labels.
  • ax.tick_params() - Style ticks and tick labels.
  • ax.grid() - Toggle or style grid lines.
  • ax.minorticks_on() - Enable minor ticks.
  • ax.minorticks_off() - Disable minor ticks.

docs.title: 4. Pyplot interface
docs.text: matplotlib.pyplot is a state-based interface that changes the current figure and axes implicitly, which makes it convenient for interactive work and simple scripts.

docs.title: 4.1 Frequently used pyplot methods
docs.text:
• plt.plot() - Plot on the current axes.
  • plt.scatter() - Scatter plot on the current axes.
  • plt.bar() - Bar chart on the current axes.
  • plt.hist() - Histogram on the current axes.
  • plt.imshow() - Show an image.
  • plt.title() - Set title on current axes.
  • plt.xlabel() - Set x label.
  • plt.ylabel() - Set y label.
  • plt.legend() - Add legend.
  • plt.grid() - Add or style grid.
  • plt.xlim() - Set x limits.
  • plt.ylim() - Set y limits.
  • plt.tight_layout() - Improve subplot spacing.
  • plt.show() - Display the figure.
  • plt.savefig() - Save the current figure.

docs.title: 4.2 When pyplot works well
docs.text:
• Quick exploratory plots.
  • Interactive sessions and notebooks.
  • Small scripts with a single figure or simple layouts.

docs.title: 5. Artists
docs.text: Everything visible in Matplotlib is an Artist, and artists are usually attached to an Axes or a Figure. Helper methods such as ax.plot() or ax.text() create artist objects for you automatically.

docs.title: 5.1 Common artist categories
docs.text:
• Line2D - created by ax.plot().
  • Text - created by titles, labels, and ax.text().
  • Patch - rectangles, circles, polygons, and similar shapes.
  • Collection - groups of objects such as scatter points.
  • Image - created by ax.imshow().
  • Legend - created by ax.legend().

docs.title: 5.2 Artist methods
docs.text:
• artist.set_visible() - Show or hide an artist.
  • artist.remove() - Remove the artist from the axes or figure.
  • ax.add_artist() - Add an artist manually to an axes.
  • fig.add_artist() - Add an artist directly to a figure.

docs.title: 5.3 Example with patch artist
docs.text:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
circle = mpatches.Circle((0.5, 0.5), 0.25, color="steelblue")
ax.add_artist(circle)
ax.set_xlim(0, 1)
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
plt.show()

docs.title: 6. Layout management
docs.text: Matplotlib supports multiple ways to arrange axes, from simple subplot grids to more manual placement.

docs.title: 6.1 Layout methods
docs.text:
• plt.subplots() - Create a regular grid of axes.
  • fig.add_subplot() - Add axes by subplot index.
  • fig.add_axes() - Add axes at exact figure coordinates.
  • plt.tight_layout() - Automatically adjust spacing.
  • layout="constrained" - Use constrained layout for better spacing.
  • fig.subplots_adjust() - Manually adjust subplot margins and spacing.
  • ax.inset_axes() - Create inset axes.

docs.title: 6.2 Example grid layout
docs.text:
fig, axs = plt.subplots(nrows=2, ncols=2, figsize=(8, 5), layout="constrained")
axs.plot(, )[1][2][3][4][5]
axs.scatter(, )[2][3][1]
axs.bar(["A", "B", "C"], )[4][6][7][1]
axs.hist()[3][1][2]

docs.title: 7. Styling and appearance
docs.text: Matplotlib includes style sheets and property-based customization for lines, markers, text, patches, and figure appearance.

docs.title: 7.1 Global styling
docs.text:
• plt.style.use() - Apply a style sheet.
  • mpl.rcParams[...] - Set global defaults.
  • mpl.rc() - Configure groups of rc parameters.

docs.title: 7.2 Common plot styling arguments
docs.text:
• color
  • linewidth
  • linestyle
  • marker
  • markersize
  • alpha
  • label
  • facecolor
  • edgecolor

docs.title: 7.3 Example style usage
docs.text:
plt.style.use("ggplot")
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(,, color="darkred", linewidth=2, marker="o")[1][2][3][4]

docs.title: 8. Text and annotation
docs.text: Text elements are artists, and annotations can be placed in data, axes, or figure coordinate systems depending on the transform used.

docs.title: 8.1 Text methods
docs.text:
• ax.text() - Add text at data coordinates by default.
  • fig.text() - Add text in figure coordinates by default.
  • ax.annotate() - Add annotation with optional arrow.
  • ax.set_title() - Add title.
  • ax.set_xlabel() - Add x-axis label.
  • ax.set_ylabel() - Add y-axis label.

docs.title: 8.2 Annotation example
docs.text:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(, )[2][3][1]
ax.annotate("Peak", xy=(1, 3), xytext=(1.3, 3.5),
            arrowprops={"arrowstyle": "->"})

docs.title: 9. Legends and colorbars
docs.text: Legends explain plotted artists, while colorbars communicate the mapping between data values and colors for images, scatter plots, and other mappables.

docs.title: 9.1 Legend methods
docs.text:
• ax.legend() - Add an axes-level legend.
  • fig.legend() - Add a figure-level legend.
  • ax.get_legend_handles_labels() - Retrieve handles and labels.

docs.title: 9.2 Colorbar methods
docs.text:
• fig.colorbar() - Add a colorbar to a figure.
  • plt.colorbar() - Add a colorbar using pyplot state.

docs.title: 10. Transforms and coordinates
docs.text: Matplotlib uses built-in transforms such as data coordinates, axes coordinates, and figure coordinates to position artists precisely. ax.transData uses data space, ax.transAxes maps the axes from 0 to 1, and fig.transFigure maps the figure from 0 to 1.

docs.title: 10.1 Important transforms
docs.text:
• ax.transData - Data coordinate system.
  • ax.transAxes - Axes coordinate system from (0, 0) to (1, 1).
  • fig.transFigure - Figure coordinate system from (0, 0) to (1, 1).
  • matplotlib.transforms - Utilities for custom transforms.

docs.title: 10.2 Example with axes coordinates
docs.text:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(, )[3][4][1][2]
ax.text(0.95, 0.95, "Top right", transform=ax.transAxes,
        ha="right", va="top")

docs.title: 11. Common plot types
docs.text: The gallery and examples section shows a wide range of plot types, including line, scatter, bar, histogram, image, contour, and statistical graphics.

docs.title: 11.1 Basic statistical and scientific plots
docs.text:
• ax.plot() - Line plots.
  • ax.scatter() - Scatter plots.
  • ax.bar() / ax.barh() - Bar charts.
  • ax.hist() - Histograms.
  • ax.boxplot() - Boxplots.
  • ax.violinplot() - Violin plots.
  • ax.errorbar() - Error-bar plots.
  • ax.fill_between() - Confidence or area fills.

docs.title: 11.2 Matrix and raster-like plots
docs.text:
• ax.imshow() - Render a 2D array as an image.
  • ax.matshow() - Matrix display.
  • ax.pcolormesh() - Pseudocolor mesh.
  • ax.contour() - Contour lines.
  • ax.contourf() - Filled contours.

docs.title: 12. Saving and exporting
docs.text: Matplotlib figures can be saved to files with figure-level or pyplot-level save functions.

docs.title: 12.1 Export methods
docs.text:
• fig.savefig() - Save the current figure to a file.
  • plt.savefig() - Save via pyplot.

docs.title: 12.2 Common export options
docs.text:
fig.savefig("plot.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("plot.pdf")
fig.savefig("plot.svg")

docs.title: 13.1 Simple line chart
docs.text:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)
y = np.sin(x)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.plot(x, y, label="sin(x)")
ax.set_title("Sine function")
ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.grid(True)
ax.legend()
plt.show()

docs.title: 13.2 Scatter plot with color mapping
docs.text:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.random.rand(100)
y = np.random.rand(100)
c = np.random.rand(100)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
sc = ax.scatter(x, y, c=c, cmap="viridis")
fig.colorbar(sc, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Colored scatter plot")
plt.show()

docs.title: 13.3 Multi-panel figure
docs.text:
fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 6), layout="constrained")
for ax in axs.flat:
    ax.plot(, )[4][1][2][3]
    ax.grid(True)

docs.title: 14. Quick reference table
docs.text: Area Key objects or methods Purpose Figure creation plt.figure(), plt.subplots(), fig.add_axes() Create figure containers and axes. Plotting ax.plot(), ax.scatter(), ax.bar(), ax.imshow() Draw data in different visual forms. Labels ax.set_title(), ax.set_xlabel(), ax.set_ylabel() Add titles and axis labels. Layout layout="constrained", plt.tight_layout(), fig.subplots_adjust() Control spacing and arrangement. Styling plt.style.use(), mpl.rcParams Apply visual themes and defaults. Artists ax.add_artist(), artist.remove() Work directly with drawable objects. Transforms ax.transData, ax.transAxes, fig.transFigure Position items in different coordinate systems. Export fig.savefig(), plt.savefig() Save outputs to image or vector files.

docs.title: 1. Core concepts
docs.text: NumPy is the foundation of numerical computing in Python. It provides fast multi-dimensional arrays and a large collection of functions for mathematical, logical, and linear algebra operations.

docs.title: 1.1 Main objects
docs.text:
• ndarray: the core NumPy array type.
  • dtype: the data type of array elements.
  • shape: the size of each dimension.
  • ndim: the number of dimensions.
  • size: total number of elements.

docs.title: 1.2 Common imports
docs.text: import numpy as np

docs.title: 1.3 Basic array example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array()[11][12][13][14]
print(arr)
print(arr.shape)
print(arr.dtype)

docs.title: 2. Array creation
docs.text: NumPy offers many ways to create arrays from lists, ranges, and generated values.

docs.title: 2.1 Common creation functions
docs.text:
• np.array() - Create an array from a list or tuple.
  • np.asarray() - Convert input to an array if needed.
  • np.arange() - Create values in a range.
  • np.linspace() - Create evenly spaced values.
  • np.logspace() - Create values spaced on a log scale.
  • np.zeros() - Create an array filled with zeros.
  • np.ones() - Create an array filled with ones.
  • np.empty() - Create an uninitialized array.
  • np.full() - Create an array filled with a constant value.
  • np.eye() - Create an identity matrix.
  • np.identity() - Create an identity matrix.
  • np.diag() - Create or extract a diagonal.

docs.title: 2.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

a = np.array()[12][13][11]
b = np.zeros((2, 3))
c = np.ones((3, 3))
d = np.eye(4)
e = np.arange(0, 10, 2)
f = np.linspace(0, 1, 5)

docs.title: 3. Array attributes
docs.text: Array attributes describe structure, type, and memory layout.

docs.title: 3.1 Common attributes
docs.text:
• arr.shape - Dimensions of the array.
  • arr.ndim - Number of dimensions.
  • arr.size - Total number of elements.
  • arr.dtype - Element data type.
  • arr.itemsize - Size of one element in bytes.
  • arr.nbytes - Total bytes consumed.
  • arr.T - Transpose of the array.
  • arr.real - Real part of complex values.
  • arr.imag - Imaginary part of complex values.
  • arr.flat - 1D iterator over elements.

docs.title: 3.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array([, ], dtype=np.int32)[13][14][11][12]
print(arr.shape)
print(arr.ndim)
print(arr.size)
print(arr.dtype)
print(arr.T)

docs.title: 4. Indexing and slicing
docs.text: NumPy supports fast indexing, slicing, boolean masking, and fancy indexing.

docs.title: 4.1 Basic methods and patterns
docs.text:
• arr[0] - Access first element.
  • arr[start:stop:step] - Slice values.
  • arr[:, 0] - Select first column.
  • arr[mask] - Select values using boolean mask.
  • arr[[0, 2, 4]] - Fancy indexing.
  • np.where() - Return positions or conditional values.
  • np.take() - Select elements by indices.
  • np.put() - Assign values at indices.

docs.title: 4.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array()[15][16][17]
print(arr[1:4])
print(arr[])[14][12]
print(arr[arr > 25])

docs.title: 5. Reshaping arrays
docs.text: Reshaping changes the array shape without changing the underlying data when possible.

docs.title: 5.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• arr.reshape() - Change array shape.
  • arr.resize() - Resize the array in place.
  • arr.ravel() - Return flattened view when possible.
  • arr.flatten() - Return flattened copy.
  • arr.squeeze() - Remove size-1 dimensions.
  • np.expand_dims() - Add a new axis.
  • np.newaxis - Add axis by indexing.

docs.title: 5.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.arange(12)
mat = arr.reshape(3, 4)
flat = mat.ravel()

docs.title: 6. Joining and splitting
docs.text: NumPy provides functions to combine arrays or split them into pieces.

docs.title: 6.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• np.concatenate() - Join arrays along an axis.
  • np.stack() - Join arrays along a new axis.
  • np.vstack() - Stack arrays vertically.
  • np.hstack() - Stack arrays horizontally.
  • np.dstack() - Stack arrays depth-wise.
  • np.column_stack() - Stack arrays as columns.
  • np.row_stack() - Stack arrays as rows.
  • np.split() - Split arrays into equal parts.
  • np.array_split() - Split arrays into uneven parts.
  • np.hsplit() - Split horizontally.
  • np.vsplit() - Split vertically.

docs.title: 6.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

a = np.array()[11][12]
b = np.array()[13][14]
c = np.concatenate([a, b])
d = np.stack([a, b])

docs.title: 7. Mathematical operations
docs.text: NumPy supports element-wise arithmetic and many mathematical functions.

docs.title: 7.1 Common operations
docs.text:
• + - Addition.
  • - - Subtraction.
  • * - Multiplication.
  • / - Division.
  • // - Floor division.
  • % - Modulo.
  • ** - Power.
  • np.add() - Add arrays.
  • np.subtract() - Subtract arrays.
  • np.multiply() - Multiply arrays.
  • np.divide() - Divide arrays.
  • np.power() - Raise to a power.
  • np.sqrt() - Square root.
  • np.abs() - Absolute value.
  • np.exp() - Exponential.
  • np.log() - Natural logarithm.
  • np.log10() - Base-10 logarithm.
  • np.sin() - Sine.
  • np.cos() - Cosine.
  • np.tan() - Tangent.

docs.title: 7.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

a = np.array()[12][11][13]
b = np.array()[18][19][14]

print(a + b)
print(a * b)
print(np.sqrt(b))

docs.title: 8. Aggregation and statistics
docs.text: NumPy includes many functions for summary statistics.

docs.title: 8.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.sum() - Sum values.
  • np.mean() - Average.
  • np.median() - Median value.
  • np.min() - Minimum value.
  • np.max() - Maximum value.
  • np.argmax() - Index of maximum.
  • np.argmin() - Index of minimum.
  • np.std() - Standard deviation.
  • np.var() - Variance.
  • np.percentile() - Percentile value.
  • np.quantile() - Quantile value.
  • np.ptp() - Peak-to-peak range.
  • np.cumsum() - Cumulative sum.
  • np.cumprod() - Cumulative product.
  • np.diff() - Differences between values.

docs.title: 8.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array()[14][18][11][12][13]
print(np.mean(arr))
print(np.std(arr))
print(np.cumsum(arr))

docs.title: 9. Comparison and logic
docs.text: Logical and comparison operations are useful for filtering and conditional processing.

docs.title: 9.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.equal() - Element-wise equality.
  • np.not_equal() - Element-wise inequality.
  • np.greater() - Greater than.
  • np.greater_equal() - Greater than or equal.
  • np.less() - Less than.
  • np.less_equal() - Less than or equal.
  • np.logical_and() - Logical AND.
  • np.logical_or() - Logical OR.
  • np.logical_not() - Logical NOT.
  • np.logical_xor() - Logical XOR.
  • np.any() - Check if any values are True.
  • np.all() - Check if all values are True.

docs.title: 9.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array()[18][11][12][13][14]
mask = arr > 3
print(arr[mask])

docs.title: 10. Random sampling
docs.text: NumPy provides the random module for generating random numbers and sampling.

docs.title: 10.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.random.rand() - Uniform random values in [0, 1).
  • np.random.randn() - Standard normal random values.
  • np.random.randint() - Random integers.
  • np.random.random() - Random floats in [0, 1).
  • np.random.choice() - Random sample from a sequence.
  • np.random.shuffle() - Shuffle an array in place.
  • np.random.permutation() - Return a shuffled permutation.
  • np.random.seed() - Set random seed.

docs.title: 10.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
x = np.random.randint(0, 10, size=5)
print(x)

docs.title: 11. Linear algebra
docs.text: The numpy.linalg submodule supports matrix operations and linear systems.

docs.title: 11.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.linalg.inv() - Matrix inverse.
  • np.linalg.det() - Determinant.
  • np.linalg.solve() - Solve linear system.
  • np.linalg.eig() - Eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
  • np.linalg.svd() - Singular value decomposition.
  • np.linalg.norm() - Vector or matrix norm.
  • np.linalg.matrix_rank() - Matrix rank.
  • np.dot() - Dot product or matrix multiplication.
  • np.matmul() - Matrix multiplication.
  • @ - Matrix multiplication operator.
  • np.trace() - Trace of matrix.

docs.title: 11.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

A = np.array([, ])[11][12][13]
b = np.array()[12][11]

x = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
print(x)

docs.title: 12. Polynomial functions
docs.text: NumPy includes polynomial utilities for fitting and evaluation.

docs.title: 12.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.poly1d() - Polynomial object.
  • np.polyfit() - Fit polynomial coefficients.
  • np.polyval() - Evaluate polynomial.
  • np.roots() - Find polynomial roots.

docs.title: 12.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

x = np.array()[13][11][12]
y = np.array()[20][18][11][13]

coef = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)
print(coef)

docs.title: 13. Sorting and searching
docs.text: NumPy provides efficient tools for ordering arrays and finding values.

docs.title: 13.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.sort() - Sort array values.
  • np.argsort() - Indices that sort the array.
  • np.partition() - Partial sorting.
  • np.argpartition() - Indices for partial sorting.
  • np.searchsorted() - Find insertion position.
  • np.unique() - Return unique values.
  • np.bincount() - Count occurrences of integers.
  • np.histogram() - Compute histogram.

docs.title: 13.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array()[14][18][11][13]
print(np.sort(arr))
print(np.unique(arr))

docs.title: 14. NaN and finite values
docs.text: NumPy has dedicated functions for handling missing or invalid numeric values.

docs.title: 14.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.isnan() - Check for NaN.
  • np.isfinite() - Check for finite values.
  • np.isinf() - Check for infinity.
  • np.nanmean() - Mean ignoring NaN.
  • np.nanmin() - Minimum ignoring NaN.
  • np.nanmax() - Maximum ignoring NaN.
  • np.nanstd() - Standard deviation ignoring NaN.
  • np.nan_to_num() - Replace NaN and infinity.
  • np.ma.masked_array() - Create masked arrays.

docs.title: 14.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array([1, np.nan, 3])
print(np.nanmean(arr))

docs.title: 15. File input and output
docs.text: NumPy can save and load arrays in binary and text formats.

docs.title: 15.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.save() - Save array in .npy format.
  • np.load() - Load .npy files.
  • np.savez() - Save multiple arrays in one file.
  • np.savez_compressed() - Save compressed multiple arrays.
  • np.savetxt() - Save array to text file.
  • np.loadtxt() - Load text file.
  • np.genfromtxt() - Load text with missing values.

docs.title: 15.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array()[11][12][13]
np.save("array.npy", arr)
loaded = np.load("array.npy")

docs.title: 16. Iteration and vectorization
docs.text: Vectorized operations are one of NumPy’s biggest strengths and usually outperform Python loops.

docs.title: 16.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• np.nditer() - Efficient iterator over array elements.
  • arr.flat - Flat iterator.
  • np.vectorize() - Wrap a Python function for array-like use.
  • np.fromiter() - Build array from iterator.

docs.title: 16.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array([, ])[12][13][14][11]
for x in np.nditer(arr):
    print(x)

docs.title: 17. Broadcasting
docs.text: Broadcasting lets NumPy operate on arrays with different shapes when rules allow it.

docs.title: 17.1 Common patterns
docs.text:
• Array + scalar - Apply scalar to every element.
  • Array + vector - Broadcast along matching dimensions.
  • np.broadcast_to() - Expand array logically without copying.

docs.title: 17.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array([, ])[19][18][13][14][11][12]
vec = np.array()[15]
print(arr + vec)

docs.title: 18. Set operations
docs.text: NumPy provides set-like functions for arrays.

docs.title: 18.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.intersect1d() - Intersection of unique values.
  • np.union1d() - Union of unique values.
  • np.setdiff1d() - Set difference.
  • np.setxor1d() - Symmetric difference.
  • np.in1d() - Membership test for 1D arrays.

docs.title: 18.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

a = np.array()[13][11][12]
b = np.array()[14][12][13]
print(np.intersect1d(a, b))

docs.title: 19. Common utility functions
docs.text: These helpers support array conversion, shape inspection, and working with values.

docs.title: 19.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• np.asarray() - Convert to array without copying when possible.
  • np.asanyarray() - Convert while preserving subclasses.
  • np.atleast_1d() - Ensure at least 1D.
  • np.atleast_2d() - Ensure at least 2D.
  • np.atleast_3d() - Ensure at least 3D.
  • np.squeeze() - Remove size-1 dimensions.
  • np.repeat() - Repeat elements.
  • np.tile() - Construct an array by repeating input.
  • np.clip() - Clip values to a range.
  • np.round() - Round values.
  • np.floor() - Floor values.
  • np.ceil() - Ceil values.
  • np.trunc() - Truncate values.

docs.title: 19.2 Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array([1.2, 2.7, 3.5])
print(np.round(arr))
print(np.clip(arr, 1.5, 3.0))

docs.title: 20.1 Basic numeric workflow
docs.text:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array()[18][11][12][13][14]
print(arr.mean())
print(arr.std())
print(arr[arr > 2])

docs.title: 20.2 2D matrix workflow
docs.text:
import numpy as np

A = np.array([, ])[11][12][13][14]
B = np.array([, ])[21][19][20][18]

C = A + B
D = A @ B
print(C)
print(D)

docs.title: 20.3 Random data analysis
docs.text:
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(0)
data = np.random.normal(loc=0, scale=1, size=1000)
print(np.mean(data))
print(np.percentile(data, ))[17][22]

docs.title: 21. Quick reference table
docs.text: Area Key methods Purpose Creation array(), zeros(), ones(), arange() Build arrays. Attributes shape, ndim, dtype, size Inspect structure. Indexing slicing, boolean masks, fancy indexing Select elements. Reshaping reshape(), ravel(), flatten() Change array layout. Math add(), multiply(), sqrt(), log() Perform numeric operations. Stats sum(), mean(), std(), percentile() Summarize data. Linear algebra inv(), solve(), eig(), svd() Matrix computations. Random randint(), random(), choice() Generate random values. Sorting sort(), argsort(), unique() Order and search data. I/O save(), load(), savetxt() Store and retrieve arrays.

docs.title: 22. Example full workflow
docs.text:
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
data = np.random.randint(0, 100, size=(5, 4))
row_mean = data.mean(axis=1)
col_sum = data.sum(axis=0)
normalized = (data - data.min()) / (data.max() - data.min())

print(data)
print(row_mean)
print(col_sum)
print(normalized)

docs.title: 1. Core concepts
docs.text: OpenCV is a computer vision library built around images as NumPy arrays and provides modules for image processing, video I/O, and GUI display. The most common Python entry point is cv2.

docs.title: 1.1 Main modules
docs.text:
• core: basic data structures and operations.
  • imgproc: image processing functions.
  • highgui: display windows and simple UI.
  • videoio: camera and video file input/output.

docs.title: 1.2 Common imports
docs.text:
import cv2 as cv
import numpy as np

docs.title: 1.3 Basic image read-display example
docs.text:
import cv2 as cv

img = cv.imread("image.jpg")
cv.imshow("Image", img)
cv.waitKey(0)
cv.destroyAllWindows()

docs.title: 2. Image I/O
docs.text: OpenCV provides simple methods to read, write, and inspect images in many formats.

docs.title: 2.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.imread() - Read an image from disk.
  • cv.imwrite() - Save an image to disk.
  • cv.haveImageReader() - Check if OpenCV can read a given file.
  • cv.haveImageWriter() - Check if OpenCV can write a given file.
  • cv.imdecode() - Decode image from a memory buffer.
  • cv.imencode() - Encode image to memory buffer.

docs.title: 2.2 Read flags
docs.text:
• cv.IMREAD_COLOR - Read as BGR color.
  • cv.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE - Read as grayscale.
  • cv.IMREAD_UNCHANGED - Preserve alpha channel and original format.

docs.title: 2.3 Example
docs.text:
img = cv.imread("photo.jpg", cv.IMREAD_COLOR)
gray = cv.imread("photo.jpg", cv.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE)
cv.imwrite("gray_output.png", gray)

docs.title: 3. Display and GUI
docs.text: HighGUI handles simple windows, keyboard input, mouse interaction, and image display.

docs.title: 3.1 Common GUI methods
docs.text:
• cv.imshow() - Show an image in a window.
  • cv.waitKey() - Wait for key press.
  • cv.destroyAllWindows() - Close all windows.
  • cv.destroyWindow() - Close one window.
  • cv.namedWindow() - Create a named window.
  • cv.resizeWindow() - Resize a window.
  • cv.setMouseCallback() - Register mouse event handler.
  • cv.createTrackbar() - Create a trackbar.
  • cv.getTrackbarPos() - Read trackbar value.

docs.title: 3.2 Example
docs.text:
import cv2 as cv

img = cv.imread("image.jpg")
cv.namedWindow("Viewer", cv.WINDOW_NORMAL)
cv.imshow("Viewer", img)
cv.waitKey(0)
cv.destroyAllWindows()

docs.title: 4. Basic image operations
docs.text: Images are usually stored as NumPy arrays, so slicing, indexing, and arithmetic operations are very common.

docs.title: 4.1 Basic operations
docs.text:
• cv.add() - Saturated addition.
  • cv.subtract() - Saturated subtraction.
  • cv.multiply() - Multiply arrays.
  • cv.divide() - Divide arrays.
  • cv.bitwise_and() - Bitwise AND.
  • cv.bitwise_or() - Bitwise OR.
  • cv.bitwise_xor() - Bitwise XOR.
  • cv.bitwise_not() - Bitwise NOT.
  • cv.addWeighted() - Weighted image blending.
  • cv.copyMakeBorder() - Add border around an image.

docs.title: 4.2 Example
docs.text:
img1 = cv.imread("a.jpg")
img2 = cv.imread("b.jpg")
blend = cv.addWeighted(img1, 0.7, img2, 0.3, 0)
cv.imwrite("blend.png", blend)

docs.title: 5. Color spaces
docs.text: OpenCV supports many color conversions, which are essential for preprocessing and segmentation.

docs.title: 5.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.cvtColor() - Convert between color spaces.
  • cv.split() - Split channels.
  • cv.merge() - Merge channels.

docs.title: 5.2 Common conversion codes
docs.text:
• cv.COLOR_BGR2GRAY
  • cv.COLOR_BGR2RGB
  • cv.COLOR_RGB2BGR
  • cv.COLOR_BGR2HSV
  • cv.COLOR_BGR2LAB
  • cv.COLOR_BGR2YCrCb

docs.title: 5.3 Example
docs.text:
img = cv.imread("image.jpg")
gray = cv.cvtColor(img, cv.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
hsv = cv.cvtColor(img, cv.COLOR_BGR2HSV)

docs.title: 6. Filtering and smoothing
docs.text: Filtering is used to remove noise, smooth images, and enhance features.

docs.title: 6.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.blur() - Mean blur.
  • cv.GaussianBlur() - Gaussian smoothing.
  • cv.medianBlur() - Median filter.
  • cv.bilateralFilter() - Edge-preserving smoothing.
  • cv.filter2D() - Apply a custom kernel.
  • cv.boxFilter() - Normalize or unnormalized box filter.

docs.title: 6.2 Example
docs.text:
img = cv.imread("image.jpg")
blur = cv.GaussianBlur(img, (5, 5), 0)
median = cv.medianBlur(img, 5)

docs.title: 7. Thresholding and masking
docs.text: Thresholding separates pixels into classes and is often used before contour extraction or segmentation.

docs.title: 7.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.threshold() - Apply global threshold.
  • cv.adaptiveThreshold() - Adaptive thresholding.
  • cv.inRange() - Create binary mask for a value range.
  • cv.compare() - Compare arrays.
  • cv.normalize() - Scale values into a range.

docs.title: 7.2 Example
docs.text:
img = cv.imread("image.jpg", cv.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE)
_, thresh = cv.threshold(img, 127, 255, cv.THRESH_BINARY)

docs.title: 8. Morphological operations
docs.text: Morphology is useful for cleaning masks, connecting regions, and removing noise.

docs.title: 8.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.erode() - Erode foreground.
  • cv.dilate() - Dilate foreground.
  • cv.morphologyEx() - Apply advanced morphology.
  • cv.getStructuringElement() - Create a kernel.

docs.title: 8.2 Morphology operations
docs.text:
• cv.MORPH_OPEN - Erosion followed by dilation.
  • cv.MORPH_CLOSE - Dilation followed by erosion.
  • cv.MORPH_GRADIENT - Difference between dilation and erosion.
  • cv.MORPH_TOPHAT - Difference between input and opening.
  • cv.MORPH_BLACKHAT - Difference between closing and input.

docs.title: 8.3 Example
docs.text:
kernel = cv.getStructuringElement(cv.MORPH_RECT, (3, 3))
opened = cv.morphologyEx(thresh, cv.MORPH_OPEN, kernel)

docs.title: 9. Geometric transforms
docs.text: Geometric operations are used for resizing, rotating, warping, and perspective correction.

docs.title: 9.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.resize() - Resize image.
  • cv.rotate() - Rotate by fixed angles.
  • cv.warpAffine() - Apply affine transform.
  • cv.warpPerspective() - Apply perspective transform.
  • cv.getRotationMatrix2D() - Create rotation matrix.
  • cv.getAffineTransform() - Create affine transform matrix.
  • cv.getPerspectiveTransform() - Create perspective transform matrix.
  • cv.remap() - Apply arbitrary mapping.
  • cv.flip() - Flip image.
  • cv.transpose() - Transpose image.

docs.title: 9.2 Example
docs.text:
img = cv.imread("image.jpg")
resized = cv.resize(img, (800, 600))
rot_mat = cv.getRotationMatrix2D((400, 300), 30, 1.0)
rotated = cv.warpAffine(resized, rot_mat, (800, 600))

docs.title: 10. Edge detection
docs.text: Edge detection helps identify boundaries and structural changes in an image.

docs.title: 10.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.Canny() - Canny edge detector.
  • cv.Sobel() - Sobel derivative.
  • cv.Scharr() - Scharr derivative.
  • cv.Laplacian() - Laplacian edge detection.

docs.title: 10.2 Example
docs.text:
img = cv.imread("image.jpg", cv.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE)
edges = cv.Canny(img, 100, 200)

docs.title: 11. Contours and shape analysis
docs.text: Contours are curves joining boundary points of objects in binary images. They are useful for object detection, shape analysis, and measurement.

docs.title: 11.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.findContours() - Find contours in a binary image.
  • cv.drawContours() - Draw contours on an image.
  • cv.contourArea() - Compute contour area.
  • cv.arcLength() - Compute contour perimeter.
  • cv.approxPolyDP() - Approximate contour shape.
  • cv.boundingRect() - Get axis-aligned bounding rectangle.
  • cv.minAreaRect() - Get minimum rotated rectangle.
  • cv.minEnclosingCircle() - Smallest enclosing circle.
  • cv.convexHull() - Compute convex hull.
  • cv.fitEllipse() - Fit ellipse to points.
  • cv.moments() - Compute image moments.

docs.title: 11.2 Example
docs.text:
img = cv.imread("shapes.png")
gray = cv.cvtColor(img, cv.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
_, thresh = cv.threshold(gray, 127, 255, cv.THRESH_BINARY)

contours, hierarchy = cv.findContours(thresh, cv.RETR_TREE, cv.CHAIN_APPROX_SIMPLE)
cv.drawContours(img, contours, -1, (0, 255, 0), 2)

docs.title: 11.3 Contour feature example
docs.text:
for cnt in contours:
    area = cv.contourArea(cnt)
    perimeter = cv.arcLength(cnt, True)
    approx = cv.approxPolyDP(cnt, 0.02 * perimeter, True)

docs.title: 12. Drawing functions
docs.text: OpenCV includes functions for drawing geometric primitives and annotating images.

docs.title: 12.1 Common drawing methods
docs.text:
• cv.line() - Draw a line.
  • cv.circle() - Draw a circle.
  • cv.rectangle() - Draw a rectangle.
  • cv.polylines() - Draw polyline.
  • cv.fillPoly() - Fill polygons.
  • cv.putText() - Draw text.
  • cv.arrowedLine() - Draw an arrow line.

docs.title: 12.2 Example
docs.text:
img = np.zeros((400, 400, 3), dtype=np.uint8)
cv.rectangle(img, (50, 50), (200, 200), (0, 255, 0), 2)
cv.circle(img, (300, 100), 40, (255, 0, 0), -1)
cv.putText(img, "OpenCV", (50, 350), cv.FONT_HERSHEY_SIMPLEX, 1, (255, 255, 255), 2)

docs.title: 13. Video I/O
docs.text: Video processing is handled by videoio, which supports cameras, video files, and frame-by-frame processing.

docs.title: 13.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.VideoCapture() - Open a camera or video file.
  • cv.VideoWriter() - Write video files.
  • VideoCapture.read() - Read the next frame.
  • VideoCapture.isOpened() - Check if capture is available.
  • VideoCapture.get() - Read capture properties.
  • VideoCapture.set() - Set capture properties.
  • VideoWriter.write() - Write a video frame.
  • VideoWriter.release() - Release the writer or capture.

docs.title: 13.2 Example
docs.text:
cap = cv.VideoCapture(0)
while cap.isOpened():
    ret, frame = cap.read()
    if not ret:
        break
    cv.imshow("Camera", frame)
    if cv.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord("q"):
        break

cap.release()
cv.destroyAllWindows()

docs.title: 14. Feature detection and matching
docs.text: OpenCV provides feature detectors and matchers for tracking, stitching, and recognition tasks.

docs.title: 14.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.ORB_create() - ORB feature detector and descriptor.
  • cv.SIFT_create() - SIFT feature detector.
  • cv.AKAZE_create() - AKAZE feature detector.
  • cv.BFMatcher() - Brute-force descriptor matcher.
  • cv.FlannBasedMatcher() - FLANN matcher.
  • cv.drawMatches() - Visualize feature matches.
  • cv.cornerHarris() - Harris corner detection.
  • cv.goodFeaturesToTrack() - Detect strong corners.

docs.title: 14.2 Example
docs.text:
img1 = cv.imread("img1.jpg", cv.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE)
img2 = cv.imread("img2.jpg", cv.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE)

orb = cv.ORB_create()
kp1, des1 = orb.detectAndCompute(img1, None)
kp2, des2 = orb.detectAndCompute(img2, None)

bf = cv.BFMatcher(cv.NORM_HAMMING, crossCheck=True)
matches = bf.match(des1, des2)
matches = sorted(matches, key=lambda x: x.distance)

docs.title: 15. Object detection utilities
docs.text: OpenCV includes many classic detection tools and pretrained cascade classifiers.

docs.title: 15.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.CascadeClassifier() - Load Haar cascade classifier.
  • CascadeClassifier.detectMultiScale() - Detect objects at multiple scales.
  • cv.HOGDescriptor() - HOG-based detector.
  • HOGDescriptor.detectMultiScale() - Detect objects with HOG.
  • cv.dnn.readNet() - Load deep learning model.
  • cv.dnn.blobFromImage() - Create input blob for DNN.
  • net.forward() - Run inference.

docs.title: 15.2 Example
docs.text:
face_cascade = cv.CascadeClassifier(cv.data.haarcascades + "haarcascade_frontalface_default.xml")
img = cv.imread("people.jpg")
gray = cv.cvtColor(img, cv.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)

faces = face_cascade.detectMultiScale(gray, scaleFactor=1.1, minNeighbors=5)
for (x, y, w, h) in faces:
    cv.rectangle(img, (x, y), (x + w, y + h), (0, 255, 0), 2)

docs.title: 16. Image pyramids and filtering
docs.text: These methods are used for multiscale analysis and image enhancement.

docs.title: 16.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.pyrUp() - Upsample image.
  • cv.pyrDown() - Downsample image.
  • cv.Laplacian() - Laplacian pyramid step.
  • cv.filter2D() - Apply custom convolution kernel.

docs.title: 16.2 Example
docs.text:
img = cv.imread("image.jpg")
down = cv.pyrDown(img)
up = cv.pyrUp(down)

docs.title: 17. File storage and parameters
docs.text: OpenCV also supports structured storage, configuration, and parameter handling.

docs.title: 17.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• cv.FileStorage() - Read or write XML/YAML files.
  • cv.getBuildInformation() - Show build configuration.
  • cv.setNumThreads() - Set OpenCV thread count.
  • cv.getNumThreads() - Get thread count.

docs.title: 18.1 Edge detection pipeline
docs.text:
import cv2 as cv

img = cv.imread("image.jpg")
gray = cv.cvtColor(img, cv.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
blur = cv.GaussianBlur(gray, (5, 5), 0)
edges = cv.Canny(blur, 100, 200)
cv.imwrite("edges.png", edges)

docs.title: 18.2 Contour extraction pipeline
docs.text:
import cv2 as cv

img = cv.imread("shapes.png")
gray = cv.cvtColor(img, cv.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
_, thresh = cv.threshold(gray, 127, 255, cv.THRESH_BINARY)
contours, hierarchy = cv.findContours(thresh, cv.RETR_TREE, cv.CHAIN_APPROX_SIMPLE)

output = img.copy()
cv.drawContours(output, contours, -1, (0, 255, 0), 2)
cv.imwrite("contours.png", output)

docs.title: 18.3 Face detection pipeline
docs.text:
import cv2 as cv

face_cascade = cv.CascadeClassifier(cv.data.haarcascades + "haarcascade_frontalface_default.xml")
img = cv.imread("people.jpg")
gray = cv.cvtColor(img, cv.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
faces = face_cascade.detectMultiScale(gray, 1.1, 5)

for (x, y, w, h) in faces:
    cv.rectangle(img, (x, y), (x + w, y + h), (0, 255, 0), 2)

cv.imwrite("faces_detected.png", img)

docs.title: 19. Quick reference table
docs.text: Area Key methods Purpose Image I/O cv.imread(), cv.imwrite() Read and save images. Display cv.imshow(), cv.waitKey() Show images in windows. Color conversion cv.cvtColor() Convert between color spaces. Filtering cv.GaussianBlur(), cv.medianBlur(), cv.filter2D() Smooth or enhance images. Thresholding cv.threshold(), cv.adaptiveThreshold() Create binary masks. Morphology cv.erode(), cv.dilate(), cv.morphologyEx() Clean and refine masks. Geometry cv.resize(), cv.warpAffine(), cv.warpPerspective() Transform images. Edges cv.Canny(), cv.Sobel() Detect boundaries. Contours cv.findContours(), cv.drawContours() Extract and visualize shapes. Video cv.VideoCapture(), cv.VideoWriter() Process camera and video files. Features cv.ORB_create(), cv.BFMatcher() Detect and match image features. Detection cv.CascadeClassifier(), cv.dnn.readNet() Detect objects or run deep models.

docs.title: 20. Example full workflow
docs.text:
import cv2 as cv

img = cv.imread("input.jpg")
gray = cv.cvtColor(img, cv.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
blur = cv.GaussianBlur(gray, (5, 5), 0)
edges = cv.Canny(blur, 50, 150)

contours, hierarchy = cv.findContours(edges, cv.RETR_EXTERNAL, cv.CHAIN_APPROX_SIMPLE)
result = img.copy()
cv.drawContours(result, contours, -1, (0, 255, 0), 2)

cv.imwrite("opencv_result.png", result)

docs.title: 1. Core concepts
docs.text: Pandas is a Python library for working with tabular, time series, and labeled data. The main data structures are Series for 1D data and DataFrame for 2D tabular data.

docs.title: 1.1 Main objects
docs.text:
• Series: one-dimensional labeled data.
  • DataFrame: two-dimensional labeled table.
  • Index: row labels.
  • Columns: column labels.

docs.title: 1.2 Common imports
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

docs.title: 1.3 Basic DataFrame example
docs.text:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "city": ["Berlin", "Köln", "Munich"],
    "population": [3.7, 1.1, 1.5]
})
df

docs.title: 2. Creating data
docs.text: Pandas can create data structures from dictionaries, lists, arrays, CSV files, Excel files, SQL tables, and more.

docs.title: 2.1 Common constructors
docs.text:
• pd.DataFrame() - Create a DataFrame.
  • pd.Series() - Create a Series.
  • pd.Index() - Create an Index.
  • pd.array() - Create a pandas array.
  • pd.Categorical() - Create categorical data.

docs.title: 2.2 File reading methods
docs.text:
• pd.read_csv() - Read CSV files.
  • pd.read_excel() - Read Excel files.
  • pd.read_json() - Read JSON files.
  • pd.read_html() - Read tables from HTML.
  • pd.read_sql() - Read SQL query results.
  • pd.read_parquet() - Read Parquet files.
  • pd.read_feather() - Read Feather files.
  • pd.read_pickle() - Read pickle files.
  • pd.read_clipboard() - Read tabular clipboard data.

docs.title: 2.3 Example
docs.text: df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")

docs.title: 3. Inspecting data
docs.text: Inspection methods help you understand structure, columns, data types, and missing values quickly.

docs.title: 3.1 Common methods and attributes
docs.text:
• df.head() - Show first rows.
  • df.tail() - Show last rows.
  • df.sample() - Random sample of rows.
  • df.shape - Number of rows and columns.
  • df.columns - Column labels.
  • df.index - Index labels.
  • df.dtypes - Data types of columns.
  • df.info() - Concise summary.
  • df.describe() - Descriptive statistics.
  • df.memory_usage() - Memory usage of columns.

docs.title: 3.2 Example
docs.text:
df.head(5)
df.info()
df.describe()

docs.title: 4. Selecting data
docs.text: Pandas provides several ways to select rows, columns, and subsets.

docs.title: 4.1 Column selection
docs.text:
• df["col"] - Select a single column.
  • df[["col1", "col2"]] - Select multiple columns.
  • df.filter() - Filter columns by name or pattern.

docs.title: 4.2 Row selection
docs.text:
• df.loc[] - Label-based selection.
  • df.iloc[] - Position-based selection.
  • df.at[] - Fast scalar access by label.
  • df.iat[] - Fast scalar access by position.

docs.title: 4.3 Example
docs.text:
df["population"]
df[["city", "population"]]
df.loc[0:2, ["city", "population"]]
df.iloc[0:2, 0:2]

docs.title: 5. Filtering data
docs.text: Filtering is used to keep rows that satisfy logical conditions.

docs.title: 5.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.query() - Filter with expressions.
  • df.isin() - Test membership.
  • df.where() - Replace values that do not meet condition.
  • df.mask() - Mask values that meet condition.

docs.title: 5.2 Example
docs.text:
df[df["population"] > 2]
df.query("population > 2")
df[df["city"].isin(["Berlin", "Munich"])]

docs.title: 6. Sorting and ranking
docs.text: Sorting methods help organize data by values or by index.

docs.title: 6.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.sort_values() - Sort by one or more columns.
  • df.sort_index() - Sort by index.
  • df.rank() - Assign ranks to values.
  • df.nlargest() - Return top rows by largest values.
  • df.nsmallest() - Return top rows by smallest values.

docs.title: 6.2 Example
docs.text:
df.sort_values("population", ascending=False)
df.sort_index()

docs.title: 7. Missing data
docs.text: Pandas has many tools for detecting, removing, and filling missing values.

docs.title: 7.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.isna() - Detect missing values.
  • df.isnull() - Same as isna().
  • df.notna() - Detect non-missing values.
  • df.dropna() - Remove missing values.
  • df.fillna() - Fill missing values.
  • df.interpolate() - Fill missing values by interpolation.
  • df.ffill() - Forward fill.
  • df.bfill() - Backward fill.

docs.title: 7.2 Example
docs.text:
df.isna()
df.dropna()
df.fillna(0)

docs.title: 8. Data cleaning
docs.text: Cleaning methods help rename, reindex, replace, and deduplicate data.

docs.title: 8.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.rename() - Rename columns or index labels.
  • df.replace() - Replace values.
  • df.drop() - Drop rows or columns.
  • df.drop_duplicates() - Remove duplicate rows.
  • df.duplicated() - Detect duplicates.
  • df.set_index() - Set a column as the index.
  • df.reset_index() - Reset the index.
  • df.astype() - Convert data types.
  • df.convert_dtypes() - Infer better dtypes.

docs.title: 8.2 Example
docs.text:
df = df.rename(columns={"population": "pop"})
df = df.drop_duplicates()
df = df.set_index("city")

docs.title: 9. Column operations
docs.text: Pandas makes it easy to create, modify, transform, and combine columns.

docs.title: 9.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.assign() - Create new columns.
  • df.pop() - Remove and return a column.
  • df.insert() - Insert a column at a position.
  • df.apply() - Apply a function along an axis.
  • df.map() - Map values in a Series.
  • df.transform() - Transform values and keep shape.
  • df.clip() - Limit values to bounds.
  • df.round() - Round numeric values.
  • df.abs() - Absolute values.
  • df.cumsum() - Cumulative sum.
  • df.cumprod() - Cumulative product.
  • df.diff() - Difference between rows.

docs.title: 9.2 Example
docs.text:
df["density"] = df["population"] / df["area"]
df = df.assign(pop_km2=df["population"] / df["area"])

docs.title: 10. Statistics and aggregation
docs.text: Pandas includes many methods for descriptive statistics and summary calculations.

docs.title: 10.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.sum() - Sum values.
  • df.mean() - Mean value.
  • df.median() - Median value.
  • df.min() - Minimum value.
  • df.max() - Maximum value.
  • df.count() - Count non-missing values.
  • df.std() - Standard deviation.
  • df.var() - Variance.
  • df.quantile() - Quantile values.
  • df.corr() - Correlation matrix.
  • df.cov() - Covariance matrix.
  • df.agg() - Aggregate with one or more functions.

docs.title: 10.2 Example
docs.text:
df["population"].mean()
df[["population", "area"]].corr()
df.agg({"population": ["sum", "mean"], "area": "mean"})

docs.title: 11. Grouping data
docs.text: Grouping is one of the most powerful pandas features for summarizing data by category.

docs.title: 11.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.groupby() - Group by one or more keys.
  • grouped.sum() - Sum within groups.
  • grouped.mean() - Mean within groups.
  • grouped.agg() - Multiple aggregations.
  • grouped.size() - Size of each group.
  • grouped.count() - Count non-missing values.
  • grouped.transform() - Transform within groups.
  • grouped.apply() - Apply custom function to each group.
  • grouped.filter() - Filter groups by condition.

docs.title: 11.2 Example
docs.text:
grouped = df.groupby("region")
grouped["population"].sum()
grouped.agg({"population": "mean", "area": "sum"})

docs.title: 12. Reshaping data
docs.text: Reshaping changes the structure of data without changing the underlying information.

docs.title: 12.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.pivot() - Reshape from long to wide.
  • df.pivot_table() - Create pivot table with aggregation.
  • pd.melt() - Unpivot wide to long.
  • df.stack() - Pivot columns into rows.
  • df.unstack() - Pivot index levels into columns.
  • df.explode() - Expand list-like values into rows.
  • df.swaplevel() - Swap index levels.
  • df.reorder_levels() - Reorder MultiIndex levels.

docs.title: 12.2 Example
docs.text:
wide = df.pivot(index="city", columns="year", values="population")
long = pd.melt(df, id_vars=["city"], value_vars=["2020", "2021"])

docs.title: 13. Combining datasets
docs.text: Pandas supports joins, merges, concatenation, and alignment of multiple tables.

docs.title: 13.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• pd.concat() - Concatenate objects along an axis.
  • df.merge() - Database-style join.
  • df.join() - Join on index or keys.
  • df.combine_first() - Update missing values from another object.
  • df.update() - Modify in place with non-missing values.
  • df.align() - Align two objects on labels.

docs.title: 13.2 Example
docs.text:
result = df1.merge(df2, on="id", how="left")
combined = pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=0)

docs.title: 14. Time series
docs.text: Pandas has strong support for datetime, period, and timedelta data.

docs.title: 14.1 Common functions
docs.text:
• pd.to_datetime() - Convert to datetime.
  • pd.to_timedelta() - Convert to timedelta.
  • pd.date_range() - Create date index.
  • pd.period_range() - Create period index.
  • pd.timedelta_range() - Create timedelta range.
  • df.resample() - Resample time series data.
  • df.rolling() - Rolling window calculations.
  • df.expanding() - Expanding window calculations.
  • df.shift() - Shift values by periods.
  • df.asfreq() - Convert to a different frequency.
  • df.tz_localize() - Localize timezone.
  • df.tz_convert() - Convert timezone.

docs.title: 14.2 Example
docs.text:
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
df = df.set_index("date")
monthly = df.resample("M").mean()

docs.title: 15. Strings and text
docs.text: String methods are accessed through Series.str and are useful for cleaning and parsing text.

docs.title: 15.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• str.lower()
  • str.upper()
  • str.strip()
  • str.contains()
  • str.replace()
  • str.split()
  • str.extract()
  • str.startswith()
  • str.endswith()
  • str.len()
  • str.cat()

docs.title: 15.2 Example
docs.text: df["city"] = df["city"].str.strip().str.title()

docs.title: 16. Categoricals
docs.text: Categorical data is efficient for repeated labels and ordered categories.

docs.title: 16.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.astype("category") - Convert to categorical.
  • pd.Categorical() - Create categorical arrays.
  • cat.categories - Category labels.
  • cat.codes - Integer codes.
  • cat.add_categories() - Add categories.
  • cat.remove_categories() - Remove categories.
  • cat.reorder_categories() - Reorder categories.
  • cat.as_ordered() - Set ordered categorical.

docs.title: 16.2 Example

docs.title: 17. Input and output
docs.text: Pandas can write data to many file formats.

docs.title: 17.1 Common output methods
docs.text:
• df.to_csv() - Write CSV.
  • df.to_excel() - Write Excel.
  • df.to_json() - Write JSON.
  • df.to_html() - Write HTML.
  • df.to_sql() - Write to SQL table.
  • df.to_parquet() - Write Parquet.
  • df.to_feather() - Write Feather.
  • df.to_pickle() - Write pickle.
  • df.to_markdown() - Write markdown table.
  • df.to_clipboard() - Copy to clipboard.

docs.title: 17.2 Example
docs.text:
df.to_csv("output.csv", index=False)
df.to_markdown()

docs.title: 18. Window functions
docs.text: Window functions support rolling, expanding, and exponentially weighted calculations.

docs.title: 18.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.rolling() - Rolling window.
  • df.expanding() - Expanding window.
  • df.ewm() - Exponential weighted window.

docs.title: 18.2 Example

docs.title: 19. Plotting
docs.text: Pandas integrates with Matplotlib for quick plotting.

docs.title: 19.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.plot() - General plotting interface.
  • df.plot.line() - Line plot.
  • df.plot.bar() - Bar plot.
  • df.plot.hist() - Histogram.
  • df.plot.box() - Box plot.
  • df.plot.scatter() - Scatter plot.
  • df.plot.area() - Area plot.
  • df.plot.pie() - Pie chart.

docs.title: 19.2 Example

docs.title: 20. Utility methods
docs.text: These methods help with checking structure, copying, and testing transformations.

docs.title: 20.1 Common methods
docs.text:
• df.copy() - Copy a DataFrame.
  • df.equals() - Check equality.
  • df.compare() - Compare two DataFrames.
  • df.any() - Any true values.
  • df.all() - All true values.
  • df.empty - Check if empty.
  • df.T - Transpose.
  • df.squeeze() - Reduce dimensions when possible.
  • df.pipe() - Chain operations cleanly.

docs.title: 20.2 Example
docs.text:
result = (
    df.copy()
      .query("population > 1")
      .assign(density=lambda x: x["population"] / x["area"])
      .sort_values("density", ascending=False)
)

docs.title: 21.1 Clean and summarize
docs.text:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")
df = df.drop_duplicates()
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"], errors="coerce")
summary = df.groupby("region").agg({"population": "sum", "area": "sum"})

docs.title: 21.2 Merge two tables
docs.text: merged = df1.merge(df2, on="id", how="left")

docs.title: 21.3 Reshape and plot
docs.text:
pivot = df.pivot_table(index="year", columns="region", values="value", aggfunc="mean")
pivot.plot()

docs.title: 22. Quick reference table
